17	# DDx Domain Principles
18	
19	> For internal engineering principles, see [docs/dev/engineering-principles.md](../../dev/engineering-principles.md).
20	
21	## Preface: Factory-Floor Design Choices
22	
23	DDx is a document-driven software factory. The product it ships is not a
24	single application — it is the machinery that lets specifications, beads,
25	agents, and harnesses combine to produce software at a predictable cost and
26	quality. The principles below are factory-floor design choices: they describe
27	how the line is laid out, where quality is inspected, which inputs are
28	trusted, and how the work moves from intent to merged code.
29	
30	Each principle has a headline, a supporting paragraph that explains the
31	choice and the constraint behind it, and a "DDx response" that names the
32	mechanism in this codebase that enacts it. The principles are grounded in
33	the research syntheses RSCH-001 through RSCH-010, which capture the
34	evidence and prior art behind each choice.
35	
36	## Principles
37	
38	### Spec-first development
39	
40	Specifications, not code, are the durable artifact in a document-driven
41	factory. Code is a perishable rendering of a spec into a particular
42	language, framework, and runtime — regenerable, replaceable, and often
43	shorter-lived than the requirement it serves. When the spec is the source
44	of truth, AI agents and human contributors share a stable target, reviews
45	focus on intent rather than syntax, and refactors stop being existential.
46	The alternative — code-first prompting where requirements live in chat
47	history and PR descriptions — produces software no one can confidently
48	change, because the "why" decays the moment the conversation scrolls. A
49	factory built on specs treats every implementation as a re-pour from the
50	mold; a factory built on code treats every change as archaeology. DDx
51	chooses the mold.
52	
53	**DDx response:** HELIX frame artifacts (vision, PRD, feature specs,
54	principles) are first-class documents in `docs/helix/`, versioned with the
55	code, and referenced by every downstream bead via `ddx.depends_on`.
56	
57	### Executable specifications
58	
59	A spec that cannot be checked is a wish. Executable specifications close
60	the gap between intent and verification by attaching machine-readable
61	acceptance criteria, contract tests, or schema constraints to every
62	requirement, so that "done" is a function the system can compute rather
63	than a judgment a reviewer must render. This matters most in an
64	agent-driven factory, where dozens of attempts may target the same bead
65	and a human cannot personally adjudicate each one. Executable specs let
66	the factory grade its own output: tests pass or fail, contracts hold or
67	break, schemas validate or reject. The cost is up-front rigor — writing
68	the check before the code — and the payoff is that quality scales with
69	throughput instead of degrading under it. DDx treats unverifiable
70	acceptance criteria as a defect in the spec, not a tolerance in review.
71	
72	**DDx response:** Bead acceptance criteria are written as concrete,
73	checkable statements (commands, file predicates, test names); the
74	post-merge review step grades each AC item against the working tree.
75	
76	### Audit trail required
77	
78	Every artifact the factory produces must be traceable to the inputs that
79	caused it: which spec, which bead, which agent, which model, which
80	prompt, which commit. Without that chain, debugging becomes guesswork and
81	compliance becomes impossible. An audit trail is also the substrate for
82	learning — you cannot improve a process whose decisions are unrecorded.
83	In an agent-heavy pipeline this is doubly true, because the agents
84	themselves are non-deterministic and their behavior drifts as models
85	change. The trail must capture not only what was decided but what was
86	considered and rejected, so that a future maintainer (human or agent) can
87	reconstruct the reasoning rather than re-derive it. DDx treats the audit
88	trail as load-bearing infrastructure, not as logging — it is part of the
89	product, not an operational courtesy.
90	
91	**DDx response:** `.ddx/executions/` preserves per-attempt evidence
92	(prompts, diffs, review findings); bead history records every state
93	transition; commits reference the bead id; agent runs log model, harness,
94	and cost.
95	
96	### Context is king
97	
98	An agent's output is bounded by the context it receives. The same model,
99	given a sharper brief, produces dramatically better work — and the same
100	model, given a bloated or stale brief, produces confidently wrong work.
101	In a document-driven factory, context engineering is not a tuning detail;
102	it is the primary lever on quality and cost. That means investing in the
103	mechanisms that select, shape, and bound what each agent sees: the spec
104	excerpts, the relevant code, the prior attempts, the review findings,
105	the persona. It also means resisting the temptation to dump everything
106	into the prompt "just in case," because irrelevant context degrades
107	performance and inflates cost. The factory's job is to assemble the
108	right brief for the right step, every time, and to make that assembly
109	visible and tunable.
110	
111	**DDx response:** Personas, harness configs, governing-artifact
112	resolution, and review-finding threading shape the brief; bead
113	descriptions are the contract; `.ddx/executions/` captures what was
114	actually sent.
115	
116	### Work is a DAG
117	
118	Work is not a list. Tasks have prerequisites, blockers, and downstream
119	consequences, and pretending otherwise produces either false serial
120	ordering (slow) or chaotic parallelism (broken). A directed acyclic
121	graph is the honest data structure: each unit of work names what must
122	exist before it starts and what becomes possible once it finishes. In a
123	factory where multiple agents run in parallel, the DAG is also the
124	scheduler — the queue dispatches what is ready and holds what is
125	blocked, without anyone having to remember the order. Modeling work as
126	a DAG forces dependencies into the open, where they can be inspected,
127	broken, or parallelized; modeling work as a list hides them, where they
128	re-emerge as merge conflicts and rework. DDx commits to the graph.
129	
130	**DDx response:** Beads have explicit `depends_on` edges; the queue
131	surfaces ready/blocked partitions; `ddx bead dep tree` renders the DAG;
132	execution respects topological order.
133	
134	### Right-size the model
135	
136	Models are not interchangeable, and using the strongest model for every
137	step is the most expensive way to be mediocre. Cheap models do well on
138	narrow, well-specified tasks; strong models earn their keep on review,
139	synthesis, and ambiguous judgment; deterministic checks (linters, type
140	systems, tests) outperform every model on the things they cover. A
141	well-run factory tiers the work: the cheapest tool that can do the job,
142	escalating only when it cannot. This is not just cost optimization — it
143	is also a quality strategy, because deterministic checks catch what
144	models miss, and strong models catch what cheap models miss. The
145	ordering matters: deterministic at the top of the ladder, then review
146	agents, then implementation agents. DDx routes by capability and budget,
147	not by brand loyalty.
148	
149	**DDx response:** Harness configs declare capability tiers; execute-loop
150	escalates on review failure; deterministic checks (tests, lints) gate
151	merge before any model gets a vote.
152	
153	### Avoid vendor lock-in
154	
155	Model vendors change pricing, deprecate models, and rewrite APIs on
156	their own schedule. A factory that bakes a single vendor's name into
157	its routing logic, prompts, or contracts hands that vendor a veto over
158	the factory's roadmap. The defense is abstraction at the right seam:
159	endpoints, capabilities, and protocols rather than provider names; live
160	discovery rather than hardcoded model lists; portable prompt formats
161	rather than vendor-specific control tokens. The cost is a layer of
162	indirection and the discipline to resist convenient shortcuts. The
163	payoff is the freedom to swap, mix, and tier providers as the market
164	moves — and to run local models alongside hosted ones without rewriting
165	the harness. DDx treats portability as a non-negotiable, even when a
166	single-vendor path would be cheaper to ship this quarter.
167	
168	**DDx response:** Endpoint-first routing (no named-provider profiles),
169	fuzzy model match against live discovery, harness abstraction over
170	provider SDKs, MCP for tool surfaces.
171	
172	### Drift is debt
173	
174	Specifications, code, configs, and documentation drift apart whenever
175	one is changed without the others. Each instance of drift is a small
176	debt: the next reader has to reconcile the conflict, the next agent has
177	to guess which is authoritative, the next change has to pay interest on
178	both. Left untreated, drift compounds until the documents are decorative
179	and the truth lives only in the code — at which point spec-first
180	development has quietly collapsed into code-first development. The
181	factory's response is not heroic vigilance; it is mechanical detection.
182	Drift checks belong in CI, in review, and in the alignment step, so
183	that divergence shows up as a failing build rather than a future
184	surprise. DDx invests in alignment tooling because the alternative is
185	slow rot, and slow rot is the failure mode that kills document-driven
186	projects.
187	
188	**DDx response:** `helix align` reconciles spec and implementation;
189	ratchets and lints detect drift in CI; the review step flags AC items
190	the implementation no longer satisfies.
191	
192	### Least privilege for agents
193	
194	Agents are powerful, fallible, and fast — a combination that punishes
195	permissive defaults. An agent with shell access, network access, and
196	write access to the entire repo can do enormous damage in seconds, and
197	the damage may not be obvious until much later. Least privilege treats
198	agent permissions the way a security-conscious system treats user
199	permissions: grant the minimum needed for the task, scope it to the
200	artifact at hand, and audit what was actually used. In practice that
201	means isolated worktrees, explicit allowlists for shell and network,
202	bounded tool surfaces, and merge gates that re-validate the diff a
203	human would re-validate. The goal is not to make agents weak; it is to
204	make their blast radius proportional to the trust we have actually
205	earned with them.
206	
207	**DDx response:** execute-bead runs in an isolated worktree; harnesses
208	declare permitted tools; settings.json allowlists gate Bash and MCP
209	calls; merge to base requires review.
210	
211	### Inspect and adapt
212	
213	A factory that does not measure itself cannot improve, and one that
214	measures the wrong things will optimize itself into a corner. Inspect
215	and adapt is the discipline of closing the loop: define what good
216	looks like, measure it on real runs, surface the result, and let the
217	measurement drive concrete changes to specs, harnesses, prompts, and
218	process. In an agent-heavy pipeline the metrics that matter are
219	throughput (closed beads per unit time), cost (dollars per closed bead),
220	quality (review-pass rate, regression rate), and cycle time (queue to
221	merge). The factory must expose these, not bury them, and must treat
222	unfavorable trends as signals rather than noise. DDx commits to running
223	on its own queue, paying its own bills, and adjusting when the numbers
224	say to.
225	
226	**DDx response:** Execution evidence aggregates into per-bead and
227	per-harness metrics; routine retros against the queue inform spec,
228	harness, and routing changes; the project dogfoods its own pipeline.
229	
230	## Tension Resolution
231	
232	When principles conflict, apply the one whose violation would cause the
233	worse outcome in context. Document the tension in the commit message or
234	design note.
235	
236	Common tensions:
237	
238	- **Right-size the model vs. Avoid vendor lock-in**: the cheapest model
239	  for a task is sometimes the one with the most proprietary surface.
240	  Prefer portability unless the cost gap is structural.
241	- **Context is king vs. Least privilege for agents**: richer context
242	  often means broader read access. Scope reads to the bead's governing
243	  artifacts; do not hand agents the whole repo by default.
244	- **Spec-first development vs. Inspect and adapt**: measurements
245	  sometimes contradict the spec. Treat that as a signal to evolve the
246	  spec, not to silently diverge from it.
247

1	---
2	title: Principles
3	weight: 2
4	---
5	
6	DDx is shaped by a small set of load-bearing principles. They drive the
7	trade-offs you see across the CLI, the bead tracker, and the agent service.
8	Each principle here is one we cite in real design and review decisions — if it
9	weren't, it wouldn't be on the page.
10	
11	## Platform, Not Methodology
12	
13	DDx provides primitives. Workflow tools (HELIX) and other consumers
14	provide opinions. The CLI owns the document library, bead tracker, agent
15	dispatch, personas, templates, and git sync. Phases, gates, supervisory loops,
16	and methodology validation belong in plugins.
17	
18	**In practice:** when someone proposes adding a "frame phase" command or a
19	"requirements gate" check to `ddx`, the answer is no — those live in the HELIX
20	plugin. When two workflow plugins need the same primitive (say, content
21	hashing), it gets lifted into DDx.
22	
23	## Project-Local by Default
24	
25	`ddx init` and `ddx install <plugin>` only write under `<projectRoot>`. There
26	is no home-directory state and no machine-wide opinions. The single global
27	artifact is `ddx-server`. Per-user concerns (API keys, model preferences) are
28	the user's responsibility, not DDx's install surface.
29	
30	**In practice:** a project's plugin tree lives at `<projectRoot>/.ddx/plugins/`,
31	its skills at `<projectRoot>/.claude/skills/`, its bead store inside the repo.
32	Cloning the repo gives you the whole picture; nothing on the developer's
33	laptop changes the answer.
34	
35	## Documents Are the Product
36	
37	Code is output. Documents are what you maintain. Prompts, personas, patterns,
38	templates, and specs are first-class, versioned, agent-consumable artifacts —
39	not afterthoughts you write once and forget.
40	
41	**In practice:** when a spec changes, beads are created for the work the
42	change implies. When implementation reveals the spec was wrong, the spec is
43	updated and the implementation follows. The artifact hierarchy is a set of
44	lenses, not a one-way pipeline.
45	
46	## Beads Are the Unit of Work
47	
48	Work flows through beads — self-contained items with acceptance criteria and
49	a dependency DAG. The queue is the interface between intent and execution.
50	Anything that needs to happen should be expressible as a bead; anything an
51	agent does should be against a bead.
52	
53	**In practice:** a developer noticing a bug doesn't fix it inline — they
54	`ddx bead create`, link dependencies, and either pick it up or let
55	`ddx work` drain the queue. The bead is the contract; review checks the
56	acceptance criteria, not the developer's memory of the conversation.
57	
58	## Cost-Tiered Throughput
59	
60	Optimize closed beads per dollar, not raw capability. Cheap models do; strong
61	models review; deterministic checks sit at the top of the ladder catching
62	what review missed. Routing is by capability and endpoint, not by
63	hardcoded provider name.
64	
65	**In practice:** `ddx try` runs implementation on a cheap model in an
66	isolated worktree, then routes the diff to a stronger reviewer; `ddx work`
67	drains the queue across many attempts. Failed reviews escalate to a higher
68	tier on the next attempt. The agent service discovers models from endpoints
69	rather than wiring named providers.
70	
71	## Git-Native, File-First
72	
73	Plain files. Standard git. No lock-in. Documents live in the repo, beads live
74	in the repo, plugins live in the repo. Sync is `git pull` and `git push` —
75	not a proprietary protocol.
76	
77	**In practice:** beads are JSONL on disk; you can read them with `cat`,
78	diff them, and merge them. Library documents are Markdown and YAML; you can
79	edit them in any tool. There is no DDx server you must connect to.
80	
81	## Agent-Agnostic
82	
83	Any harness with a prompt-in/output-out contract plugs into the agent
84	service. Claude, Codex, Gemini, local models — all use the same prompt
85	envelope, the same session logging, the same quorum review.
86	
87	**In practice:** `ddx agent run --harness claude` and `ddx agent run --harness
88	qwen-local` differ in cost and capability, not in interface. Routing decisions
89	are observable, swappable, and don't require touching the CLI.
90	
91	## Make Intent Explicit
92	
93	Code, configs, and documents should say what they mean. Avoid implicit
94	conventions, magic values, and behavior that depends on undocumented ordering.
95	When intent is ambiguous, name it.
96	
97	**In practice:** persona bindings are explicit in `.ddx.yml`, not inferred
98	from filenames. Bead acceptance criteria are checked items, not prose
99	expectations. A reviewer can tell what was intended without asking.
100	
101	## Prefer Reversible Decisions
102	
103	When two options are otherwise equivalent, choose the one easier to undo.
104	Commit to irreversible choices deliberately, with documented rationale.
105	
106	**In practice:** endpoint-based agent routing is preferred over hardcoded
107	provider names because endpoints are swappable. Project-local install is
108	preferred over global state because it's reversible by deleting `.ddx/`.
109	
110	## When Principles Conflict
111	
112	Apply the one whose violation would cause the worse outcome in the current
113	context. Document the tension in the commit message or design note. Common
114	tensions:
115	
116	- **Platform vs. Simplicity** — keeping plugins out of the CLI sometimes
117	  costs duplication across plugins. Accept the duplication until two plugins
118	  prove the primitive is shared infrastructure.
119	- **Cost-Tiered vs. Reversible Decisions** — routing logic that locks in a
120	  vendor is cheap to ship and expensive to undo. Prefer endpoint abstractions
121	  even when a single provider would be simpler today.
122	
123	For the canonical version of these principles (terse, contributor-facing),
124	see `docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md` in the repository.
125

8	id: RSCH-001
9	title: "Spec-First Development"
10	kind: research-synthesis
11	summary: "Specifications, not code, are the durable artifact AI agents consume; tooling that treats spec → plan → tasks as the primary workflow outperforms code-first prompting."
12	tags: [spec-driven, methodology, ddx-principle]
13	---
14	
15	# Spec-First Development
16	
17	## Principle
18	
19	The specification is the source of truth. Code is the regenerable expression of
20	a spec at a moment in time, in a particular language and framework.
21	
22	## Synthesis
23	
24	Two independent industrial efforts have converged on this inversion. GitHub's
25	Spec Kit (REF-005) ships a `/specify → /plan → /tasks` workflow that treats the
26	PRD as executable input — the spec generates the plan, the plan generates the
27	task list, and only then does code generation begin. AWS Kiro (REF-006) embeds
28	the same loop inside an IDE: natural-language requirements become structured
29	specs, design notes, and implementation steps before any code is produced. Both
30	tools share the bet that AI assistants regress to plausible-looking but
31	incorrect code when they are pointed at a vague request, and that the way to
32	discipline them is to first nail down a precise, reviewable spec.
33	
34	The evidence for the inversion is operational rather than statistical: when
35	Spec Kit and Kiro users change a requirement, the system re-derives the plan,
36	contracts, and tests rather than asking the developer to manually propagate
37	the change through stale code. This is the failure mode that traditional
38	"docs alongside code" workflows accept as inevitable — drift between intent
39	and implementation — and the inversion eliminates it by making the spec the
40	artifact that gets edited, branched, reviewed, and merged.
41	
42	The inversion only works if specs are complete enough to actually drive
43	generation. Both REF-005 and REF-006 force this by template: required
44	sections, explicit `[NEEDS CLARIFICATION]` markers when the prompt is
45	ambiguous, and gate checklists ("requirements are testable", "no speculative
46	features") that the agent must satisfy before moving on. The lesson is that
47	spec-first development is not a cultural request; it is a tooling constraint.
48	
49	## DDx Implication
50	
51	DDx adopts spec-first as a load-bearing assumption. The document library
52	(prompts, personas, templates, patterns) and the bead tracker exist precisely
53	because the things AI agents read — specs, acceptance criteria, dependency
54	edges — are first-class artifacts that need version control, dependency
55	tracking, and audit. `ddx bead create` captures intent before work starts;
56	the bead description and AC are the contract the agent executes against, the
57	same way Spec Kit's `spec.md` is the contract its task agents execute against.
58	
59	DDx does not encode any specific spec template (HELIX provides those); it
60	provides the substrate — typed artifacts, dependency edges, addressable IDs —
61	that any spec-first workflow needs to function.
62

=== RSCH-002 ===
# Executable Specifications

## Principle

A specification is only as strong as the checks it generates. Acceptance
criteria written as prose are aspirational; acceptance criteria that compile
into runnable scenarios are enforceable.

## Synthesis

Behavior-Driven Development (REF-019) was the first widely adopted attempt to
collapse the gap between "what the system should do" and "the test that
proves it does." Cucumber and its descendants take Given-When-Then prose
written by product stakeholders and bind it directly to step definitions,
turning the human-readable spec into the executable test suite. The lineage
matters because it shows the pattern is not new to the LLM era — the
discipline of *writing specs that are also tests* predates AI agents by two
decades and survived because it works.

EvalPlus (REF-018) supplies the empirical counterweight: the consequence of
*not* doing this. The authors took HumanEval and MBPP, the two benchmarks
the LLM-code-generation field had been optimizing against, and expanded
their test suites by orders of magnitude. Reported pass@1 scores collapsed —
in some cases models that "passed" 80% of HumanEval problems failed the
expanded suite on the same code. The original specs, expressed as a thin
test suite, had been overstating model correctness for years. The fix was
not better models; it was better executable checks attached to the same
spec.

Together these two references frame the principle. REF-019 shows the
mechanism (specs that compile to scenarios). REF-018 shows the cost of
weak mechanisms (benchmarks that look passed but aren't). For AI-generated
code this is acute: an agent will reliably make any prose spec it reads
look satisfied, because plausible-looking code is what LLMs produce by
default. The only durable check is one the spec itself produces.

## DDx Implication

DDx beads carry an `<acceptance>` block that is intended to be discharged by
a *specific* test, file path, or evidence artifact, not by an agent's
self-assessment. The execute-bead loop and review pipeline both treat AC
items as gates: each item must point to concrete evidence, and the review
agent re-checks against the same AC the implementing agent saw.

This is why DDx stores AC inside beads (structured, queryable) rather than
inside free-form prose: the AC needs to be machine-addressable so a later
review pass — possibly by a different model — can verify item-by-item that
the spec generated its own check. Bead AC plays the same role in DDx that
Cucumber scenarios play in BDD and that the expanded test suite plays in
EvalPlus.
=== RSCH-003 ===

# Audit Trail Required

## Principle

Every agent action — prompt, tool call, file edit, model used, cost incurred —
must be captured in an append-only, replayable log. The log, not the current
state of the working tree, is the truth.

## Synthesis

Pat Helland's "Data on the Outside vs. Data on the Inside" (REF-014) draws
the foundational distinction: data flowing between independent actors must be
immutable, identified, and versioned, because the receiver has no way to
reach back and re-read the sender's mutable internal state. Agent
interactions are exactly this case — the model that produced a diff is gone
the moment it returns; the only thing that persists is the artifact it
emitted. If that artifact is not captured with its provenance, the system
has no contract surface to reason about.

Kleppmann's *Designing Data-Intensive Applications* (REF-020) generalizes
the pattern across distributed systems: derived state in caches, replicas,
indexes, and search systems is reconstructable only if the underlying log
of changes is preserved. Stream-processing architectures, CDC pipelines,
and consensus systems all converge on the same pattern — an ordered,
append-only log is the primary artifact, and current state is a projection.

Fowler's Event Sourcing essay (REF-021) makes the architectural pattern
concrete: rather than mutating in place, record each state change as an
immutable event; the present is rebuildable from the log at any time. The
benefits Fowler enumerates — temporal queries, debugging, rebuilding
projections after a bug fix, regulatory audit — are exactly the properties
needed when an autonomous agent has been modifying production code on its
own initiative.

The composition is forceful: REF-014 says cross-actor contracts must be
immutable, REF-020 says distributed correctness depends on the log, REF-021
says the log can be the *primary* artifact rather than a debugging
afterthought. Apply this to agentic systems and the conclusion is that the
agent's action stream must be event-sourced. Anything less leaves teams
unable to answer "what did the agent do, why, and what did it cost" — which
is the bare minimum for trusting agent-modified code in a shared codebase.

## DDx Implication

DDx records every execution under `.ddx/executions/<timestamp>-<hash>/` —
the prompt the agent saw, the diff it produced, the harness and model it
used, cost, and exit reason. The bead tracker itself behaves as an
event-sourced store: bead state changes (created, blocked, ready, closed)
are durable, addressable, and replayable. The execute-bead worktree is
named, preserved on failure, and merged with `--no-ff` so the history
contains a tamper-evident record of which agent did which work. None of
this is optional polish; it is the contract surface that lets humans
trust, review, and roll back agent output.
=== RSCH-004 ===
# Context Is King

## Principle

The model is not the bottleneck; context selection and ordering are.
A smaller model with the right 4K of context will routinely beat a larger
model given 200K of irrelevant tokens.

## Synthesis

Liu et al.'s "Lost in the Middle" (REF-008) established the canonical
empirical result: across multiple frontier models and task types, recall
follows a U-shaped curve — information at the start and end of the prompt
is reliably retrieved; information in the middle is dropped or
hallucinated over. The effect is robust and reproduced across model
families. The position of evidence in the context window matters as much
as whether the evidence is there at all.

Chroma's 2025 "Context Rot" study (REF-009) extended the finding past
position effects: even when relevant information is well-placed, model
performance degrades as raw input length grows, well within advertised
context windows. Frontier models that report 200K or 1M token windows show
measurable accuracy decay long before those limits are approached. The
larger window is real capacity, but it is not free capacity — every token
spent on irrelevant material is taxing the same attention budget that the
relevant material has to compete in.

The two studies compose into a clear operating constraint: more context is
not better context. The right move is to retrieve aggressively, prune
ruthlessly, and place the load-bearing material at the start or end of the
window. "Just give the model the whole repo" is a strategy that gets
worse, not better, as context windows grow — because the rot effect
scales with how much chaff the model has to sift.

For agent systems this matters compoundingly: agents call themselves and
each other, and each call inherits or assembles its own context. A
multi-agent loop that ladles full transcripts forward at each step is
manufacturing degradation at every hop.

## DDx Implication

DDx is built on the assumption that *what an agent reads* is the unit of
quality. The document library is structured retrieval surface — typed
artifacts (specs, personas, templates, patterns), addressable IDs, and
explicit `depends_on` edges — so a bead can be assembled with exactly the
context it needs and no more. Beads carry only their description and AC
into the execute-bead prompt; governing references are pulled by ID, not
inlined wholesale. Personas (REF-008/REF-009-aware) keep system prompts
short and role-specific rather than catch-all. The architectural bet is
that small, sharp, well-ordered context beats large, loose context — and
the empirical literature backs the bet.
=== RSCH-005 ===

# Work Is a DAG

## Principle

Units of work and their dependencies form a directed acyclic graph. Treating
that graph as a first-class object — explicit, queryable, schedulable —
unlocks parallelism, incremental execution, and reasoning about the critical
path. Treating it as implicit prose produces serial bottlenecks and lost
parallelism.

## Synthesis

CPM and PERT (REF-022) introduced the pattern in 1957–1958: model a project
as nodes (tasks) and edges (dependencies), compute the longest path through
the graph, and identify which tasks are on the critical path. Seventy years
of project management practice has not produced a better abstraction. The
DuPont Polaris missile program and chemical-plant maintenance scheduling
both used the same machinery — task nodes, dependency edges, durations,
critical path — that modern issue trackers reinvent piecemeal.

Bazel (REF-023) carried the same structure into software builds. Every
build action is a node with declared inputs and outputs; the dependency
graph is hermetic and content-addressed. The payoff is operational:
incremental rebuilds touch only what changed, remote caching shares
results across the team, and the build is reproducible because the graph
fully describes the work. The structural discipline — *every dependency
declared, no implicit ones* — is what makes the rest possible.

Dask (REF-024) generalized the idea to runtime computation: arbitrary
Python work is represented as a task graph and scheduled across cores or
clusters. Independent branches run in parallel; dependent ones wait. The
scheduler does not need to understand the semantics of any task — only
the graph topology — and that is sufficient to extract parallelism that
the user did not have to design for explicitly.

The convergence is the point. Three independent fields — project
management, build systems, parallel computation — arrived at the same
abstraction because the abstraction is correct. Anything that manages
dependent units of work eventually grows into a DAG; the choice is whether
that DAG is explicit and tooled or implicit and brittle.

## DDx Implication

The DDx bead tracker is the explicit DAG for AI-assisted work. `ddx bead
dep add` declares edges; `ddx bead ready` walks the graph to find beads
whose dependencies are satisfied; `ddx bead blocked` and `ddx bead dep
tree` make the topology queryable. The execute-loop drains the ready set
and can fan out parallel agents on independent branches, the same way
Dask or Bazel do — because the graph is explicit, the scheduler does not
have to guess. Beads carry critical-path information by virtue of their
edges; humans see what is blocking what without having to reconstruct it
from issue-tracker prose.
=== RSCH-006 ===
# Right-Size the Model

## Principle

There is no one model. Different tasks have different cost/quality
tradeoffs, and the right answer is to route each call to the cheapest
model that can satisfy it, escalating only when needed.

## Synthesis

FrugalGPT (REF-015) provided the foundational empirical result: a cascade
of cheaper-to-more-expensive models, gated by a learned confidence check,
matches GPT-4-class quality on representative workloads at a fraction of
the cost. Chen, Zaharia, and Zou framed three composable techniques —
prompt adaptation, LLM approximation, and model cascades — but the
load-bearing one is the cascade. The empirical claim was strong: most
queries are cheap-model territory, the expensive model is only needed for
the residual hard tail, and a routing policy that recognizes which is
which captures the bulk of the savings.

The 2026 multi-model routing consensus (REF-029) shows the industry has
arrived at the same answer through independent paths. OpenRouter,
LiteLLM, and the major frontier vendors all expose tiered routing,
fallback chains, live capability discovery, and per-task model selection
as first-class features. The shared pattern across vendors and OSS
projects is not coincidence — it is the operationalization of FrugalGPT's
research finding into production tooling. Live discovery matters because
the model catalog churns weekly: hardcoding a model name into a workflow
ages out faster than the workflow does.

The composition: REF-015 says routing wins on cost/quality, REF-029 says
the industry has standardized on the routing surface. The conclusion is
that any AI-native platform that hardcodes a single model — or routes by
named provider profile — is locking in tomorrow's drift.

## DDx Implication

DDx treats the agent service as a routing layer, not a model wrapper.
`ddx agent run --harness=<name>` selects a harness by capability, not by
hardwired model; harnesses can themselves cascade or quorum across
models. The execute-loop runs cheap models first and escalates failed
beads to stronger models on retry — directly mirroring FrugalGPT's
cascade and the user's standing "cost-tiered work" goal. Endpoint-based
discovery (vs. named provider profiles) is the implementation of REF-029's
live-discovery requirement: the catalog is a runtime query, not a
checked-in constant. The cost record kept in `.ddx/executions/` closes
the loop by letting routing decisions be evaluated empirically rather
than asserted.
=== RSCH-007 ===
# Avoid Vendor Lock-In

## Principle

Standardize the contract; let implementations compete underneath. A
platform that takes a hard dependency on a single vendor is a platform
whose ceiling is that vendor's strategy and lifespan.

## Synthesis

The Language Server Protocol (REF-025) is the canonical recent example.
Before LSP, every editor needed bespoke integrations with every language
toolchain — an `m × n` integration problem that practically capped how
many languages an editor could support and how many editors a language
could reach. By standardizing a JSON-RPC contract between editor
("client") and language tooling ("server"), Microsoft collapsed the
problem to `m + n`. The result was an explosion of supported
language/editor combinations, none of which had to be implemented by any
single vendor. The contract was the leverage; the implementations
fell into place once the contract was stable.

The portability lineage (REF-030) shows the pattern is decades older and
domain-independent. POSIX (1988) made operating-system services portable
across UNIX vendors by standardizing the syscall and shell contract; the
implementations diverged radically (AIX, Solaris, Linux, macOS) but
portable software kept working. SQL (ISO/IEC 9075, 1987) did the same
for relational databases — the dialects argue endlessly, but the core
contract is durable enough that an application can swap engines without
rewriting its data layer. OCI (2015) standardized container image and
runtime contracts and broke Docker's de facto monopoly almost
immediately, enabling Podman, containerd, and a Kubernetes ecosystem
that no longer depended on a single vendor's roadmap.

What all four standards share: they specify *interfaces*, not
implementations, and they were authored to be implementable by anyone.
That is the structural property that produces durability. Standards
that smuggle implementation choices into the contract — by mandating a
specific data format, network library, or configuration system —
collapse back into vendor lock-in by the back door.

## DDx Implication

DDx is a protocol-shaped system on purpose. The bead tracker has a JSONL
import/export surface compatible with `bd`/`br`. The agent service speaks
to harnesses through a typed RunOptions/Output contract, not to vendor
SDKs. The MCP integration uses an open protocol, not a proprietary
interface. The endpoint-first routing redesign explicitly replaces named
provider profiles with discovery against a generic endpoint contract —
the same move LSP made against editor-specific integrations. The bet,
backed by REF-025 and REF-030, is that the projects that survive the
next decade of AI tooling churn are the ones whose contract surfaces are
stable enough to outlast any individual vendor's roadmap.
=== RSCH-008 ===
# Drift Is Debt

## Principle

Documentation that lives separately from the code it describes will drift
out of correctness. The fix is structural, not cultural — keep the doc and
the artifact it describes in the same regenerable unit.

## Synthesis

Knuth's "Literate Programming" (REF-017) is the foundational structural
proposal. Knuth's claim was not that programmers should write better
comments; it was that the program should be written *primarily* for human
readers, with prose and code interleaved as a single artifact, and the
machine-runnable code extracted as a derived view. The reason this
matters for drift is that there is only one source: changing the
explanation and changing the code are the same edit. There is no second
artifact to forget about.

The 2023 empirical study of documentation drift (REF-028) supplies the
data Knuth's argument predicts. Across a corpus of open-source projects,
documentation diverged from implementation at measurable rates;
different documentation types decayed at different speeds (API docs
faster than architecture docs, both faster than glossaries); and the
divergence accumulated regardless of project maturity, language, or
team size. The drift is not a discipline failure of any particular team
— it is a structural property of any system that maintains two
artifacts and asks humans to keep them in sync by hand.

The composition is direct. REF-028 says drift is universal when the doc
and the code are separate. REF-017 says the answer is to make them not
separate. Forty years of evidence has accumulated since the literate
programming proposal, and every iteration of the principle — Doxygen,
docstrings, OpenAPI generated from code, type-driven docs — has the same
shape: a single source, with the human-readable view and the
machine-runnable view both derived from it.

For AI-driven systems the stakes are higher because agents *read the
docs* to decide what to do. Drifted docs mislead the agent the same way
they mislead a new hire, except an agent acts faster and asks fewer
clarifying questions.

## DDx Implication

DDx treats specs, beads, AC, and code as parts of one regenerable
system, not as separate artifacts maintained in parallel. Beads carry
their AC inline; the AC is the test contract. The execute-bead loop
captures what the agent actually read versus what the spec said,
exposing drift the moment it is introduced. The doc audit
(`ddx doc audit`) walks `depends_on` edges and flags broken references,
which is the structural equivalent of a build error for documentation.
The product principle is that drift is not a process problem to be
solved by quarterly cleanups — it is a tooling problem to be solved by
making the divergence impossible to commit in the first place.
=== RSCH-009 ===
---

# Least Privilege for Agents

## Principle

An agent gets the smallest set of files, tools, and capabilities required
to do the current job, scoped to a specific time and location, with
elevation requiring human confirmation. The agent is treated as an
untrusted process that happens to be useful, not as a trusted teammate.

## Synthesis

Saltzer and Schroeder (REF-026) enumerated the foundational principles
in 1975: least privilege, fail-safe defaults, complete mediation,
separation of privilege. The agent case is not different in kind from
the multi-user OS case — what changed is that the "process" can now be
persuaded in natural language, by attacker-controlled input, to misuse
whatever authority it holds.

OWASP's 2025 LLM Top 10 (REF-011) catalogs the threat model: prompt
injection, insecure output handling, supply-chain risk, and — most
relevantly — *excessive agency*. Excessive agency is what happens when
an agent has more tools, permissions, or write access than the current
task requires; the agent itself does not need to be malicious for the
excess to be exploited. EchoLeak (REF-012, CVE-2025-32711) is the
canonical real-world demonstration: a zero-click prompt injection in
Microsoft 365 Copilot exfiltrated user data via attacker-crafted email
content. The agent had authority to read user mail and to render
arbitrary content; the combination was the vulnerability.

Sheridan and Verplank's levels-of-automation framework (REF-010) is the
human-side counterpart: at every level above "fully manual," there must
be a designed point at which the human confirms or overrides. The 1978
paper enumerated ten levels precisely so designers would have to
*choose* rather than slide unconsciously into full autonomy. For
agentic coding tools the choice is the same: which actions are
auto-approved, which require confirmation, which are forbidden?

The four references compose: REF-026 supplies the principle, REF-011
the modern threat catalog, REF-012 a worked example, REF-010 the
human-confirmation discipline. The conclusion is operational: scope
agent authority by default, audit continuously, require a human at
every elevation boundary.

## DDx Implication

DDx executes agents in isolated git worktrees — `execute-bead` runs in a
named worktree under `.execute-bead-wt-*`, scoping every file edit to
that branch and that bead. The agent cannot reach across worktrees,
mutate the bare repo directly, or touch unrelated branches. Tool
permissions are scoped per harness, with the executing process inheriting
only the credentials the harness declared. Persona definitions encode
role-bounded behavior. Destructive or shared-state actions (push, branch
deletion, force operations) sit behind explicit human-in-the-loop
confirmation, in line with REF-010's levels-of-automation discipline.
The architectural stance is that the agent's blast radius must be
designed, not assumed.
=== RSCH-010 ===

# Inspect and Adapt

## Principle

An agent's first output is a draft. Quality comes from a structured
evidence-review loop where a critic — same model, different model, or
deterministic check — compares the draft against a specific contract and
the draft is revised until the contract is satisfied.

## Synthesis

Self-Refine (REF-016) is the smallest demonstrable instance of the loop.
Madaan et al. showed that a single LLM, asked to generate, critique its
own output, then revise based on the critique, improves on a wide range
of tasks versus single-shot generation. The mechanism does not require
a second model — it requires a structured prompt for the critique step
(what to look for, what would count as "wrong") and a willingness to
iterate. The result generalizes because the failure mode it addresses —
first-draft plausibility errors the same model recognizes on a second
pass — is universal to LLMs.

Anthropic's "Building Effective Agents" (REF-027) operationalizes the
loop into design patterns: evaluator-optimizer pairs, orchestrator-worker
patterns, and the explicit recommendation to use the *simplest* pattern
that works. Production-grade agents are workflows of small,
evidence-checked steps — not autonomous loops with vague goals. The
loop must be anchored to concrete checkable outputs at each step;
absent that anchor, the agent drifts.

MAST (REF-013) catalogs what happens when the loop is missing. The 2025
multi-agent failure taxonomy classifies failures across real
benchmarks: coordination breakdowns, role confusion, missing
verification, termination failures. Failures cluster wherever there is
no concrete artifact for one agent to verify another against — the
loop has no evidence to inspect, so it cannot adapt. MAST is the
negative-space proof that REF-016 and REF-027 point at the right
discipline.

The composition: REF-016 shows the loop works at the single-model
level, REF-027 shows how to compose it into production workflows,
REF-013 shows what fails when inspection is absent. This is not
generic agile retrospection — it is a specific claim about
evidence-bound critique loops as the unit of agent quality.

## DDx Implication

DDx runs every bead through a generate-then-review loop. The
execute-bead pass produces a commit; the review pass re-checks against
the same `<acceptance>` items the executor saw, with each AC item
needing a specific, addressable piece of evidence. Failed reviews
reopen the bead and escalate to a stronger model, with the review
findings threaded into the retry prompt as a `<review-findings>`
section — directly mirroring the Self-Refine critique-and-revise loop
but with the critic's output preserved as durable input to the next
attempt. The multi-model `--quorum` and codex-plus-opus plan-review
discipline are the same pattern with independent critics. The
acceptance criteria are the anchor MAST identifies as load-bearing —
without them, the loop has nothing to inspect.
